title: Clinical Case Study: PTSD Presenting as Agoraphobia and Panic Disorder
content:
Patient Background: 34-year-old male veteran presenting with recurrent, unexpected panic attacks occurring in public places, leading to severe avoidance behaviors (Agoraphobia).
Initial Diagnosis: Panic Disorder with Agoraphobia. 
Clinical Course: Patient underwent standard Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT) for panic and was prescribed Alprazolam for acute attacks. Therapy stalled after 8 weeks. Upon deeper clinical interview, it was discovered that the "random" panic attacks in grocery stores (characterized by sudden sweating and tachycardia) were consistently triggered by the smell of diesel fuel from delivery trucks outside, or loud mechanical clanking sounds.
Re-evaluation: The panic attacks were actually dissociative flashbacks to combat trauma involving an IED explosion near a diesel transport vehicle. The avoidance of public spaces was trauma-related avoidance, not primary agoraphobia.
Successful Therapeutic Pathway: Treatment pivoted from Panic-focused CBT to Trauma-Focused Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (TF-CBT) and Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing (EMDR). Alprazolam was tapered off due to risk of dependency, and an SSRI (Paroxetine) was initiated focused on PTSD symptom management.
Long-term Outcome: After 12 sessions of EMDR, the physiological reactivity to diesel triggers decreased by 80%. Diagnosis revised to PTSD.
